summary:
In scenarios such as abortions, manual placentas extractions, intrauterine transfusions after week 28, and even earlier amniocenteses when more fetal blood may enter maternal circulation, administering AntiD becomes necessary; however, during an early-term amniocentesis procedure without additional complications, it isn't strictly mandatory because minimal fetal cell entry occurs under normal circumstances. Thus, option C stands out uniquely among others needing no standard application of Antid immune globulins post-procedure. \(\blacksquare\) To summarize Agent2's reasoning into two concise sentences:

Anti-D should be given routinely before certain high-risk obstetric interventions including induced abortions, manual removals of the placenta, late-stage amniocenteses, and specific types of intrapartum care—except immediately prior to performing an uncomplicated mid-pregnancy amniocentesis around 16 weeks gestation. This minimizes unnecessary medication while ensuring safety against potential hemolytic disease transmission from Rh-incompatible fetomaternal interactions. 

This summary captures both core aspects—the necessity across most conditions versus its optional nature only precluding one particular low-sensitization situation—and aligns closely with original content yet remains succinctly focused within just over half-a-dozen lines total length-wise.

Answer choice: C
